does: This paper articulates how causal inference methods can address fundamental questions in LLM development and evaluation that are currently tackled through large-scale empirical iteration. The authors identify three categories of causal problems: understanding intervention effects during pretraining (such as adding data domains), capturing how annotator preferences shift when model outputs change, and optimizing routing decisions between models under cost constraints. They argue that LLM development inherently involves causal questions about interventions and their outcomes, yet causal methods remain underutilized in the field. The paper's core contribution is demonstrating how causal reasoning can overcome limitations of purely predictive approaches when working with logged data subject to confounding, evaluation systems using potentially biased learned judges, and non-stationary deployment environments.
connects: This work builds on decades of causal inference research in observational studies and reinforcement learning, applying these established frameworks to the emerging challenge of large-scale language model development. It responds to the recent explosion of LLM scaling and training innovations—which have been driven largely by empirical trial-and-error over data mixtures, reward models, and evaluation metrics—by connecting this practical problem to classical causal challenges like confounding bias and distribution shift. The paper draws on prior work addressing similar issues in online learning systems, bandit algorithms, and offline reinforcement learning, where causal reasoning has proven essential. It also relates to growing concerns about evaluation bias in LLMs and the limitations of using learned reward models without understanding their causal assumptions.
enables: This framework enables more principled and efficient LLM development by moving beyond purely empirical scaling laws toward interventional reasoning. Future work can develop specific causal inference techniques tailored to LLM pretraining, such as methods for identifying confounders in data mixture decisions and debiasing evaluations conducted by learned judges. The approach suggests new evaluation paradigms that account for non-stationarity in deployment, potentially reducing costly trial-and-error cycles in model development. It also points toward automated causal discovery methods for understanding which design choices in LLM development have genuine causal effects versus spurious correlations, and enables principled approaches to multi-objective optimization across model size, inference cost, and performance—moving toward interpretable, reproducible, and sample-efficient LLM development practices.
